channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - insider-gaming.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for insider-gaming.com

item.title: Pro Call of Duty Players Say Battlefield 6 is a ‘Worse COD’ For Dad Gamers
item.content:encoded:
In a new clip uploaded on social media, professional Call of Duty League players from OpTic Texas have offered up their two cents on Battlefield 6. It has been a few months now since both Battlefield 6 and Black Ops 7 hit the market, and truth be told, neither game is doing too well.

Unsurprisingly, the Call of Duty League pro players are firmly in the Call of Duty camp when it comes to picking between Black Ops 7 and Battlefield 6. In their words, it’s a slower game that’s built for ‘dads’.

Is Battlefield Better than Call of Duty?

Last year’s release of Battlefield 6 was slated by some as being a potential Call of Duty ‘killer’. It’s a term often lobbied around by those desperate to see the end of Activision’s first-person shooter series. When it dropped, Battlefield 6 did remarkably well, but the success ultimately petered off, culminating in layoffs and ever-dropping sentiments about the game.

Black Ops 7 hasn’t fared much better, though.

In a new clip posted on social media, the OpTic Texas pro players, with some other organization members like Methodz and Scump, debated Battlefield vs. Call of Duty, that age-old discussion between gamers.

Brandon ‘Dashy’ Otell was quick to offer his inflammatory thoughts:

Your game sucks. Your game sucks!

A lot of their hype is them just 1v1’ing COD.

It just looks like a worst COD to me, bro. Slower… If maybe my Dad was going to play COD, he’s literally going to buy Battlefield 6.

Scump agreed with him, then H3CZ, the CEO of OpTic Gaming, explained his perspective:

I don’t like any game that prohibits my movement abilities.

To give credit to both sides of the fence, Methodz stated that they’re ultimately two different games, suggesting that it’s not entirely fair to compare them. He said that he likes Call of Duty more, but was still able to enjoy Battlefield 6.

Do you think Battlefield 6 is subjectively better than Black Ops 7? Let us know what you’re thinking on the Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more Insider Gaming coverage, check out the news that Nadeshot has addressed the aBeZy slur scandal

Senior Editor and Esports Lead

Grant has been gaming for 30+ years and in the industry for 10+. You'll probably find him playing a post-apocalyptic game or an extraction shooter somewhere.

More from Grant Taylor-Hill

MOBILE APP

item.title: Everything You Need to Know About The 2026 LCS Spring Split
item.content:encoded:
North America’s League of Legends Championship Series (LCS) returns this week and begins the road to the Mid-Season Invitational (MSI).

This year, two North American teams will qualify for MSI, though the second seeds will have to deal with the brutal new MSI Play-In format with intense competition for one single spot in the Bracket Stage.

Nonetheless, reigning LCS champions LYON had a solid showing at First Stand and showed that the region can compete internationally, though they have mistakes that need to be ironed out.

With no roster changes made since the LCS Lock In, the Spring Split is looking like a very interesting contest. Here’s all you need to know.

When Does the LCS Spring Split Start?

The second LCS split of the year will begin on Saturday, April 4, with a couple of intense best-of-threes.

The opening day will see the following best-of-three matches:

  • Cloud9 vs LYON — 1 pm PT / 10 pm CET
  • Sentinels vs Disguised  3 pm PT / 12 am CET (next day)

The first match is a rematch of the LCS Lock In Playoff finals in which LYON came out surpirse winners.

The regular season will last from April 4 until May 17, then the LCS Spring Playoffs will begin on May 23 and conclude with the final on June 14.

LCS Spring 2026 Format

Just like the LEC Spring Split, the LCS Spring Split will be played entirely of multi-game series with no best-of-ones.

The regular season will be a single round-robin of best-of-threes to determine the top six teams who will advance to the Playoffs.

The Playoffs will then see a double-elimination bracket made up entirely of best-of-fives.

The fifth and sixth seed teams will start in the lower bracket, meaning they will have no second chances and any lost series will spell elimination.

The top two will qualify for MSI, with the LCS Spring champions automatically qualifying for the main event of the international tournament.

Will anyone be able to stop LYON this split? Let us know your bold predictions on the Insider Gaming Discord.

And find out when every LoL esports Spring Split starts with the LPL, LEC, LCK, CBLoL, and LCP all starting the road to MSI.

MOBILE APP

Level Up Your Gaming News

Available on iOS & Android

item.title: Vatira Pleads for Rocket League Bot Fixes After Epic Games Layoffs
item.content:encoded:
Rocket League superstar Axel ‘Vatira’ Touret has unleashed another scathing rant about the game’s ongoing issue with bots and DDoS attacks.

The Karmine Corp pro previously ranted about this same issue back in November 2025, proclaiming “Rocket League is dead” as the issues began to even impact Rocket League’s RLCS esports LAN qualification.

The developers promised that these issues were being addressed, but recently Epic Games laid off 1,000 staff members in budget cuts, including significant employees from Rocket League developers Psyonix.

Those layoffs led to the shutdown of multiple Epic titles, including Rocket League spinoff Rocket Racing, and Vatira claims the ‘soccar’ game could soon suffer the same fate.

Vatira Calls For Action On Rocket League DDoS Attacks

In a lengthy post on his social media, Vatira described the current state of Rocket League as “dire” and explained how the esports scene is being affected:

“We’ve had to create Discord servers just to try to play competitively, but it’s not a miracle solution. It’s complicated to manage, not easy to access, and obviously we can’t gather players from all over the world.”

Bots and DDoS attackers are ruining the quality of ranked matches, with the latter forcing players to disconnect and give automatic wins to these cheaters who are rapidly climbing ranks.

“The problem is that the overall player skill level is declining and our playtime is being cut in half or even by three,” Vatira added before pleading: “Epic Games, do something right now.”

Vatira claims fewer people are watching Rocket League now, with content creators losing audiences and possibly their livelihoods. Yet, he maintains that there is still a strong interest in the esport because of how easy the game is to understand and the skill level of current pros.

Psyonix Respond to Vatira

Rocket League product manager of Pysonix and Epic Games, Murty Shah, has since responded to Vatira’s outburst.

The former Rocket League esports manager insisted that solving the bots issue is a top priority for the developers:

“Confirming I’ve seen this and that it’s top of mind for us. We’re actively working to get more anti-botting measures in ASAP, alongside the upcoming release of Easy Anti-Cheat, to make sure you guys can play without any disruptions.

“We absolutely cannot have bots impacting your practice for the RLCS. In the meantime, if you run into any bots, please keep reporting them in the Discord. We’re getting more support in there now to monitor your reports and are continuously running ban waves.”

Vatira then gave a reply of his own, remaining sceptical that progress will be made:

“I appreciate the response, but the fact remains that we’ve been seeing the same pattern for months, if not years.”

“Nothing against you, obviously, Scheist. I believe we all want Rocket League to be the best game ever,” he added.

Do you think the Epic Anti-Cheat will finally solve this issue for Rocket League? Let us know your thoughts on the Insider Gaming Discord.

In the meantime, the esports scene is going strong with teams preparing for the Rocket League Esports World Cup 2026 tournament.

MOBILE APP

item.title: Former Project Blackbird Developer Forms Eighth Lantern Studios
item.content:encoded:
When Project Blackbird was canceled by Microsoft last year, it left a lot of developers, artists, and others looking for work. While some have found work at other studios, one former developer has decided to start his own.

From the ashes of one cancelation and layoff comes Eighth Lantern Studios, a company founded by former lead encounter designer at Zenimax, Gabe Sheets.

Eighth Lantern began as a side project last year and consists of Sheets as well as several industry veterans from games like Perfect Dark, Remnant II, The Elder Scrolls Online, Call of Duty, and more.

  • More News: EXCLUSIVE: Veteran Studio Working On New Lord of the Rings Game

“At Eighth Lantern Studios, our mission is to combine our AAA experience with fresh ideas to make games that give players a reason to laugh and just have fun with their friends,” said Sheets, a 20-year veteran in the industry. “So, kinda like a finely-tuned gameplay whoopee cushion: timeless, endlessly replayable, and fun for all ages.”

At the start, the team began working on a prototype for a game while continuing to look for work. Sheets said that the belief was they would test the waters if they felt the prototype had a chance.

“Well, it turns out we all fell in love with the project, and not only that, we’re certain that it’s something worth sharing with the rest of the world,” he said.

Currently, Eighth Lantern is bootstrapping its first game, a third-person roguelite puppet shooter. You can learn more about the studio at its website and on social media.

What do you think of former AAA developers branching out and starting their own studios? Leave your thoughts in the comments below, and in this official Insider Gaming Discord.

In other news, read more exclusive details about the recent Eidos Montreal layoffs and what it means for the studio. And for even more Insider Gaming delivered directly to your inbox, sign up for our newsletter.

Mike has been covering the gaming industry since 2012, and has reported on some of the largest events in the industry while also working as an investigative reporter. Outside of…

More from Mike Straw
